project.name: radius-bundles
project.description: An embedded Shopify app for creating and managing product bundles (fixed, BOGO, BXGY, volume, mix-and-match, frequently bought together), pairing a Next.js + React admin with a Rust-compiled Shopify Function for discount calculation and a Liquid theme extension for storefront widget rendering. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

nodes.languageNotes: Custom safe_mul helper guards against u64 overflow with MAX_QUANTITY=10000; serde-driven config types model all bundle shapes.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to anchor the project's purpose: an embedded Shopify app for building and managing six kinds of product bundles. The README also lays out the three runtime surfaces you'll see throughout the tour — a Next.js admin app, a Rust-compiled Shopify discount function, and a Liquid storefront widget — plus the path aliases and bun-based tooling. Read it first so the rest of the architecture has a place to land. CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md sit alongside it as the deeper architecture map for contributors.

tour.title: Shopify App Manifest & Web Package
tour.description: Before any code runs, Shopify needs to know what the app declares. The shopify.app.*.toml manifest registers the app proxy, webhook subscriptions, OAuth scopes, and product metafield definitions, while web/package.json pins the Next.js 16 / React 19 / Prisma 7 / Polaris stack and exposes the bun scripts you'll use during the rest of the tour. Together these files form the contract between Shopify's platform and the codebase you're about to read.
tour.languageLesson: TOML manifests in Shopify CLI use top-level tables ([access_scopes], [webhooks], [app_proxy]) to declare app capabilities. Each subscription is a TOML array-of-tables; field naming follows Shopify CLI conventions and is validated by `shopify app config validate`.

tour.title: Next.js Entry Point
tour.description: web/app/layout.tsx is the root of the App Router tree: it sets metadata, mounts global providers, and wraps everything in an I18nLoader for locale-aware rendering. web/app/page.tsx is the index entry that the Shopify embed lands on. Together they bootstrap the embedded admin UI that every dashboard route inherits — every later step renders inside this layout.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js App Router uses nested layout.tsx files as the React tree's outer shell — they wrap every page.tsx beneath them. Layouts persist across navigation, so providers (React Query, Zustand, App Bridge) live here rather than in pages.

tour.title: Dashboard Routing & Route Groups
tour.description: Building on the root layout from Step 3, the (dashboard) route group organizes the merchant-facing admin pages — dashboard, bundles, analytics, settings — without adding a URL segment. Each page.tsx is a server component that renders the Polaris-based UI for one feature area; this is the thin top of the data flow stack you'll trace in later steps.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js route groups — folders wrapped in parentheses like (dashboard) — share layouts and code organisation without affecting the URL path. They are the App Router's answer to grouping related pages that should live under the same UI shell.

tour.title: Database Schema
tour.description: schema.prisma is the single source of truth for the 23 PostgreSQL models — Bundle, BundleProduct, BundleAnalytics, ABTest, Automation, AIInsight, Shop, AppSettings — plus the enums (bundle types, statuses, discount types) referenced everywhere. Read it now so when later steps mention BundleProduct.role or Bundle.volumeTiers, you already know the shape. The Prisma client generated from this file backs every repository in the codebase.
tour.languageLesson: Prisma uses a declarative schema language that compiles to a typed client. `@@index` and `@@unique` are model-level constraints; JSON-typed fields trade type safety for flexibility (used here for variantConfig, conditions, globalStyles).

tour.title: Feature Module Anatomy: Bundles
tour.description: Every domain in /web/features follows the same shape — actions, services, repositories, hooks, api, components — and the bundles feature is the canonical example. The actions/ directory exposes Next.js server actions as the API boundary; services/ holds business logic split into write/read/transformer/security/scheduler; repositories/ wraps Prisma calls. Tracing one bundle CRUD request through these three layers teaches the whole codebase's data flow.

tour.title: Server Actions to React Query
tour.description: Server actions from Step 6 are consumed on the client through a React Query layer. bundles.queries.ts defines the hooks that components call; bundles.queryKeys.ts centralises cache keys so invalidation stays consistent. This is the bridge between the UI and the service layer — every dashboard component you'll see in Step 4 ultimately reaches the database through this pattern.
tour.languageLesson: TanStack React Query 5 uses `gcTime` (formerly `cacheTime`) for inactive cache lifetime and `staleTime` for freshness windows. Centralising query keys (the queryKeys.ts pattern) makes targeted invalidation safe — `removeQueries` for inactive, `invalidateQueries` for active.

tour.title: Shopify Auth, GraphQL & Webhooks
tour.description: web/lib/shopify is the integration spine: auth/verify validates Shopify session tokens, setup wires the SDK on cold-start, and webhooks/register.ts subscribes the GDPR, app/uninstalled, products/*, orders/create, and locale handlers. Combined with the /api/auth and /api/webhooks route handlers, this is how the app talks to Shopify's platform — OAuth on install, GraphQL for data, and webhooks for change events.

tour.title: GraphQL Schema for Shopify Admin
tour.description: All Shopify Admin API calls are typed against hand-written GraphQL operations under /web/lib/graphql/schema, which @shopify/api-codegen-preset compiles into typed clients. Reading the product, discount, and metafield queries reveals exactly what the app reads and writes on Shopify's side — and how Step 6's services stay typed end-to-end.
tour.languageLesson: Shopify Admin GraphQL uses explicit fragments to share field selections between queries — the codegen step picks them up via .graphqlrc.yml and emits a single typed client. Pinned API version (2026-04) lives in shopify.app.toml and the constants module.

tour.title: Discount Engine: Rust WASM Function
tour.description: Bundle discounts cannot run in the Next.js process — Shopify enforces them at checkout via a WASM Function. cart_lines_discounts_generate_run.rs computes line-item discounts (BOGO, BXGY, percentage, fixed, custom price), and cart_delivery_options_discounts_generate_run.rs handles free shipping. The two .graphql files declare the input schema Shopify gives the function; Cargo.toml pins the wasm target. This is the only Rust in the repo, and it runs on Shopify's edge — not yours.
tour.languageLesson: Shopify Functions are Rust crates compiled to wasm32-wasip1 and uploaded as `.wasm` modules. They are pure functions invoked by Shopify with a typed GraphQL input and return a discount/operation list — no network, no filesystem, deterministic execution within a strict instruction budget.

tour.title: Storefront Widget: Liquid + Vite TS
tour.description: On the merchant's storefront, the bundle UI is rendered by a Liquid theme extension. The app-block.liquid and app-embed.liquid blocks insert containers into the theme; the radius-bundle-* snippets supply per-layout markup; and the Vite-built TS in /web/widgets/src bootstraps interactive behaviour, calls the App Proxy for product data, and dispatches to layout-specific renderers (fixed, BOGO, volume). This is how the data from Steps 5-6 finally reaches a real shopper.
tour.languageLesson: Liquid theme extensions ship blocks (merchant-placed) and embeds (theme-wide) that read merchant config from a JSON schema. Pairing Liquid for SSR markup with a Vite-bundled TS entry gives you templating-at-render-time plus client-side reactivity — a common Shopify storefront pattern.

tour.title: Settings Customizer & CI/CD
tour.description: The customizer (web/features/settings/configs/customizer.config.ts) is a metadata-driven UI: a single config object describes 4 sections, fields, presets, and which fields support per-device overrides — the components from Step 4's settings page render that config dynamically. Finally, .github/workflows/deploy.yml and vercel.json close the loop, defining how every change you've just traced gets linted, tested, and shipped to production.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions YAML uses `on` triggers, parallel `jobs`, and sequential `steps`. Caching bun/node_modules speeds up CI; matrix strategies test across versions. vercel.json overrides the framework auto-detection — useful for monorepos where the deployable app sits in /web.
